summary: Graham asks what the “best essay” would be like and concludes that, by his own criteria, it would usually be an essay that reveals a major new discovery. That reframes the problem into how to write essays well: start with a compelling question, use writing to discover ideas, and iteratively refine by strict rereading and branching toward novelty and generality. He argues that great essays depend on the quality of questions and the writer’s curiosity, breadth, and willingness to revise, with timelessness emerging only in special cases.
key_points: The best possible essay at a given time would usually present the most important new discovery available.
Writing essays well is a method of discovery: start with a question, commit to words, and refine by ruthless rereading.
Novelty and generality guide which branches of thought to pursue; rewriting allows backtracking.
Great initial questions often feel outrageous, imply breadth, and align with genuine curiosity.
Timeless essays are rare because effective ideas get absorbed into culture; breadth is the deeper aim.
money_quotes: The best essay would be on the most important topic you could tell people something surprising about.
Writing essays, at its best, is a way of discovering ideas.
Writing converts your ideas from vague to bad.
One of the most dangerous temptations in writing (and in software and painting) is to keep something that isn't right, just because it contains a few good bits or cost you a lot of effort.
If you want to surprise readers not just now but in the future as well, you have to write essays that won't stick.
key_insight: Great essays come from using writing as a discovery process, where the quality of the initial questions—and relentless refinement toward novelty and generality—determines how far the ideas can go.
